title: Cardiovascular Outcomes in Patients With Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus Treated With GLP-1 Receptor Agonists and SGLT-2 Inhibitors

items.title: Why GLP-1 receptor agonists and SGLT-2 inhibitors are preferentially used in high-risk T2DM
items.text: Numerous agents in both classes reduce important cardiovascular and renal complications when added to usual care in patients with established ASCVD or multiple risk factors; they should be used preferentially irrespective of whether additional glucose lowering is needed.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "These newer agents should be preferentially used in patients at high risk for cardio-renal complications, including those with established major comorbidities, likely irrespective of the need for additional glucose lowering or preexisting antihyperglycemic therapy."

items.title: How to incorporate SGLT-2 inhibitors into already complex diabetes regimens
items.text: When glycemic control is already at goal, background antihyperglycemic therapy may need adjustment to limit hypoglycemia and cost. Before starting an SGLT-2 inhibitor, assess blood pressure and volume status and anticipate diuretic or antihypertensive changes.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "anticipatory modification of diuretic or other antihypertensive therapy may be needed to avoid significant volume depletion or hypotension with the addition of an SGLT-2 inhibitor."

items.title: Excess cardiovascular risk persists in diabetes despite improved population outcomes
items.text: Individuals with diabetes still have approximately 2-fold excess cardiovascular risk; only 14% meet combined targets for glycemia, blood pressure, lipids, and nonsmoking status—leaving substantial residual risk addressable by proven drug classes.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "only 14% meet targets for glycemic and blood pressure control and lipid management and do not smoke cigarettes."

items.title: How FDA cardiovascular outcomes trials reshaped T2DM therapy selection
items.text: Since 2008 FDA guidance, large placebo-added-to-usual-care trials in patients with established ASCVD or high MACE risk have defined which newer antihyperglycemic classes reduce MACE, heart failure hospitalization, or renal endpoints.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "In 2008, the US FDA issued a guidance recommending thorough evaluation of all new diabetes therapies for cardiovascular safety."

items.title: DPP-4 inhibitors: neutral MACE with a saxagliptin heart-failure signal
items.text: DPP-4 inhibitor CVOTs are consistently neutral for MACE; saxagliptin in SAVOR-TIMI-53 unexpectedly increased heart failure hospitalization—a finding not reproduced elsewhere but relevant when treating patients with existing heart failure.
items.reference: DPP-4 Inhibitors: "the SAVOR TIMI-53 trial of saxagliptin therapy unexpectedly found an increase in hospitalization for heart failure in patients assigned to that medication when compared with placebo."

items.title: GLP-1 receptor agonist cardiovascular outcomes: class heterogeneity
items.text: Liraglutide, semaglutide, and dulaglutide reduce MACE in high-risk T2DM; lixisenatide and once-weekly exenatide were neutral. REWIND showed MACE benefit even when most patients lacked prior cardiovascular events. GLP-1 RAs have neutral effects on heart failure hospitalization.
items.reference: GLP-1 Receptor Agonists: "These trials have shown that liraglutide, semaglutide, and dulaglutide all reduce the risk of MACE when compared with placebo in patients with T2DM at high risk for such events."

items.title: SGLT-2 inhibitors: consistent heart failure benefit across CVOTs
items.text: Empagliflozin and canagliflozin reduce MACE; dapagliflozin did not reduce MACE in DECLARE but did reduce a co-primary heart failure endpoint. All completed SGLT-2 inhibitor CVOTs show significant heart failure outcome reduction; DAPA-HF extended benefit to HFrEF with or without diabetes.
items.reference: SGLT-2 Inhibitors: "All of the completed cardiovascular outcomes trials of SGLT-2 inhibitors have shown a significant reduction in heart failure outcomes with use of those agents."

items.title: Why renal benefits differ between GLP-1 RAs and SGLT-2 inhibitors
items.text: Meta-analyses suggest GLP-1 RA renal benefit is driven mainly by reduced macroalbuminuria, whereas SGLT-2 inhibitors reduce worsening eGFR, end-stage kidney disease, and renal death; CREDENCE established canagliflozin benefit in albuminuric CKD.
items.reference: Renal Effects of GLP-1 Receptor Agonists and SGLT-2 Inhibitors: "the renal outcomes benefit noted in the trials of GLP-1 receptor agonists were primarily attributable to a reduction in macroalbuminuria, while SGLT-2 inhibitors were found to reduce the risks of worsening estimated glomerular filtration rate, end-stage kidney disease, or death due to renal causes."

items.title: Why cardiovascular trial benefits are not explained by glucose lowering alone
items.text: GLP-1 RA benefit may reflect antiatherogenic effects via weight and blood pressure improvement; SGLT-2 inhibitor benefit likely involves hemodynamic effects of glycosuria and natriuresis—these mechanisms operate independently of A1c reduction.
items.reference: GLP-1 Receptor Agonists and SGLT-2 Inhibitors Outcomes Summary: "The cardiovascular benefits identified in the cardiovascular outcomes trials do not appear attributable to or dependent on glucose lowering."

items.title: How to choose between proven GLP-1 RA and SGLT-2 inhibitor agents
items.text: Guidelines favor drugs with proven outcomes benefit; some suggest GLP-1 RA first in ASCVD with SGLT-2 inhibitor as second choice, but without head-to-head data either proven class is reasonable based on comorbidities, eGFR, heart failure phenotype, and patient preference.
items.reference: GLP-1 Receptor Agonists and SGLT-2 Inhibitors Outcomes Summary: "there are no head-to-head trials comparing the effects of the 2 drug classes in this patient population; thus, choice of a proven agent from either class is reasonable, based on individual patient characteristics and preferences."

items.title: FDA-labeled cardiovascular indications for SGLT-2 inhibitors in T2DM
items.text: Empagliflozin reduces cardiovascular death in T2DM with established ASCVD; canagliflozin reduces MACE in that population; dapagliflozin reduces heart failure hospitalization in T2DM with established ASCVD or multiple cardiovascular risk factors.
items.reference: SGLT-2 Inhibitors: "empagliflozin is indicated to reduce the risk of cardiovascular death in adult patients with T2DM and established ASCVD; canagliflozin is indicated to reduce the risk of MACE in the same population; and dapagliflozin is indicated to reduce the risk of hospitalization for heart failure in adults with T2DM and established ASCVD or multiple cardiovascular risk factors."

items.title: Barriers limiting uptake of cardioprotective diabetes therapies
items.text: Despite guideline recommendations, implementation remains limited in the highest-benefit populations because of unfamiliarity with rapidly emerging CVOT data, concern about adverse effects, and difficulty fitting new agents into complex regimens.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "implementation of these therapies has thus far been quite limited, particularly in the populations of patients most likely to derive an outcome benefit."

items.question: A 76-year-old woman with T2DM, ASCVD (prior stent), BMI 33, BP 124/80 on lisinopril and hydrochlorothiazide, A1c 7.2%, eGFR 56, takes no glucose-lowering drugs. Best regimen change?
options: Start empagliflozin only without changing diuretic
items.explanation: Established ASCVD warrants a proven cardioprotective agent; empagliflozin is appropriate and HCTZ should be stopped when starting SGLT-2 inhibitor in a normotensive older patient to avoid volume depletion.
items.reference: Case 1: "Answer: C and D) Start empagliflozin and discontinue hydrochlorothiazide"

items.question: The same patient asks why metformin is not started first. What is the best explanation?
options: Metformin is contraindicated when A1c is below 8%
CV benefits of SGLT-2 inhibitors require background metformin
Metformin has no demonstrated efficacy for cardiovascular risk reduction in established ASCVD
Empagliflozin cannot be used without metformin
items.explanation: Outcomes trial benefits are not restricted to metformin users; metformin lacks proven CV risk reduction in patients with established ASCVD.
items.reference: Case 1: "metformin has no demonstrated efficacy in the reduction of cardiovascular risk in patients with established ASCVD."

items.question: A 57-year-old man with T2DM, peripheral and coronary artery disease, CKD (eGFR 28), A1c 8.4%, on sitagliptin and basal insulin without hypoglycemia. Best change?
items.explanation: eGFR is below empagliflozin and canagliflozin thresholds; liraglutide (MACE-beneficial GLP-1 RA) should replace sitagliptin—combined GLP-1 RA plus DPP-4 inhibitor is not recommended.
items.reference: Case 2: "Answer: E) Substitute liraglutide for sitagliptin"

items.question: A 67-year-old woman with insulin-treated T2DM, CKD, HFpEF, A1c 8.2%, no recent hypoglycemia. Best next step?
items.explanation: CREDENCE supports canagliflozin 100 mg in T2DM with albuminuric diabetic nephropathy and eGFR ≥30; it reduces kidney failure, CV death, and heart failure hospitalization.
items.reference: Case 3: "Answer: D) Add canagliflozin, 100 mg daily"

items.question: A 56-year-old man with obesity, multivessel CAD, HFrEF, prediabetes (A1c 5.9%), eGFR 46, elevated BNP. Preferred intervention?
options: Begin liraglutide titrated to 3 mg daily for weight loss
items.explanation: DAPA-HF showed dapagliflozin reduced worsening heart failure or cardiovascular death in HFrEF with or without diabetes—preferred over lifestyle or metformin alone in this phenotype.
items.reference: Case 4: "Answer: B) Begin dapagliflozin, 10 mg daily"

items.question: Which GLP-1 receptor agonist cardiovascular outcomes trial demonstrated MACE reduction in patients mostly without prior cardiovascular events?
items.explanation: REWIND enrolled high-risk patients but most lacked prior CV events yet still showed MACE benefit with dulaglutide.
items.reference: GLP-1 Receptor Agonists: "the REWIND trial of dulaglutide also enrolled patients at high risk, but in contrast to the other cardiovascular outcomes trials, most of the patients included had not had a prior cardiovascular event."

items.question: In the DECLARE trial, dapagliflozin compared with placebo showed which outcome pattern?
options: Reduced MACE but not heart failure
Neutral MACE with reduced heart failure endpoint
Reduced MACE and renal death only
items.explanation: DECLARE did not show MACE reduction but did significantly reduce a co-primary heart failure outcome.
items.reference: SGLT-2 Inhibitors: "The DECLARE trial did not find a reduction in MACE risk with dapagliflozin; however, a second primary heart failure outcome in that trial was significantly reduced with active therapy compared with placebo."

items.question: Regarding DPP-4 inhibitors and cardiovascular risk in T2DM, which statement is correct?
options: They reduce MACE and should replace GLP-1 RAs
They are neutral for MACE and should not be used solely to reduce cardiovascular risk
They consistently reduce heart failure hospitalization
Saxagliptin is preferred in patients with established heart failure
items.explanation: DPP-4 inhibitors are CV-safe but neutral; saxagliptin may increase HF hospitalization.
items.reference: DPP-4 Inhibitors: "DPP-4 inhibitors, although largely found to be safe from a cardiovascular perspective, should not be used to reduce cardiovascular risk in patients with T2DM."

items.question: Canagliflozin 100 mg daily in CREDENCE reduced which composite in T2DM with albuminuric CKD?
items.explanation: CREDENCE showed significant reduction in kidney failure and cardiovascular events at 2.62 years.
items.reference: Renal Effects of GLP-1 Receptor Agonists and SGLT-2 Inhibitors: "canagliflozin, 100 mg daily, significantly reduced the risk of kidney failure and cardiovascular events at 2.62 years compared with placebo."

items.question: Across GLP-1 RA cardiovascular outcomes trials, hospitalization for heart failure was generally:
items.explanation: GLP-1 RA trials show neutral effects on heart failure hospitalization unlike SGLT-2 inhibitors.
items.reference: GLP-1 Receptor Agonists: "the trials of GLP-1 receptor agonists have found a neutral effect of these agents on rates of heart failure hospitalization."

items.question: A patient with ASCVD has A1c at individualized goal on basal insulin without hypoglycemia. Regarding addition of a cardioprotective agent:
options: Wait until A1c exceeds 8% before adding GLP-1 RA or SGLT-2 inhibitor
Proven agents should be considered even when glycemic control is adequate
SGLT-2 inhibitors are only indicated when A1c is above 9%
items.explanation: High-risk patients should receive proven cardio-renal agents irrespective of need for additional glucose lowering.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "likely irrespective of the need for additional glucose lowering or preexisting antihyperglycemic therapy."

items.question: Before starting empagliflozin in a patient on hydrochlorothiazide who appears euovolemic with BP 124/80, what adjustment is most appropriate?
options: Discontinue hydrochlorothiazide and reassess volume status at follow-up
No antihypertensive changes are ever needed with SGLT-2 inhibitors
items.explanation: Anticipatory diuretic reduction prevents volume depletion and hypotension when initiating SGLT-2 inhibitor therapy.
items.reference: Case 1: "that medication should be discontinued (Answer D) when empagliflozin is started, and the patient's volume status and blood pressure should be reassessed at follow-up."

items.question: In the United States, which GLP-1 receptor agonist is indicated to reduce MACE in adults with T2DM and established ASCVD?
items.explanation: Among GLP-1 RAs with trial benefit, liraglutide carries a US indication to reduce MACE in T2DM with established ASCVD.
items.reference: GLP-1 Receptor Agonists: "thus far in the United States liraglutide is indicated to reduce the risk of MACE in adults with T2DM and established ASCVD."

items.statement: Individuals with diabetes have approximately 2-fold excess risk of cardiovascular outcomes compared with persons without diabetes.
items.explanation: Excess vascular risk remains substantial despite population improvements in diabetes care.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "individuals with diabetes still have an approximately 2-fold excess risk of cardiovascular outcomes (including coronary heart disease, stroke, and vascular death) compared with outcomes of persons without diabetes."

items.statement: Completed DPP-4 inhibitor cardiovascular outcomes trials have consistently shown neutral effects on MACE.
items.explanation: All four listed DPP-4 inhibitor CVOTs were neutral for MACE.
items.reference: DPP-4 Inhibitors: "Trials of the DPP-4 inhibitor medications completed thus far have consistently demonstrated a neutral effect of these drugs on rates of MACE."

items.statement: SAVOR-TIMI-53 found increased hospitalization for heart failure with saxagliptin versus placebo.
items.explanation: This signal has not been reproduced in other trials but warrants caution in patients with heart failure.
items.reference: DPP-4 Inhibitors: "the SAVOR TIMI-53 trial of saxagliptin therapy unexpectedly found an increase in hospitalization for heart failure in patients assigned to that medication when compared with placebo."

items.statement: GLP-1 receptor agonists consistently reduce hospitalization for heart failure in cardiovascular outcomes trials.
items.explanation: GLP-1 RA trials show neutral effects on heart failure hospitalization; SGLT-2 inhibitors reduce HF outcomes.
items.reference: GLP-1 Receptor Agonists: "the trials of GLP-1 receptor agonists have found a neutral effect of these agents on rates of heart failure hospitalization."

items.statement: Cardiovascular benefits of GLP-1 RAs and SGLT-2 inhibitors in outcomes trials appear dependent on glucose lowering.
items.explanation: Trial benefits operate through weight, BP, hemodynamic, and other mechanisms not explained by A1c change alone.
items.reference: GLP-1 Receptor Agonists and SGLT-2 Inhibitors Outcomes Summary: "The cardiovascular benefits identified in the cardiovascular outcomes trials do not appear attributable to or dependent on glucose lowering."

items.statement: The ELIXA trial demonstrated MACE reduction with lixisenatide.
items.explanation: Lixisenatide was neutral for cardiovascular risk in ELIXA.
items.reference: Case 2: "Lixisenatide (Answer C) was not found to reduce cardiovascular risk in the ELIXA trial"

items.statement: Empagliflozin is indicated for use when eGFR is 45 mL/min/1.73 m² or higher.
items.explanation: At eGFR 28, empagliflozin is not the correct choice per labeling discussed in Case 2.
items.reference: Case 2: "Empagliflozin (Answer B) is not currently the correct choice for this patient given his low estimated glomerular filtration rate, as this medication is at present only indicated for use in patients with an estimated glomerular filtration rate of 45 mL/min per 1.73 m² or higher."

items.statement: Dapagliflozin in DAPA-HF reduced worsening heart failure or cardiovascular death in patients with HFrEF with or without diabetes.
items.explanation: DAPA-HF extended SGLT-2 inhibitor heart failure benefit beyond the diabetes CVOT population.
items.reference: SGLT-2 Inhibitors: "dapagliflozin therapy significantly reduced the risk of worsening heart failure or death from cardiovascular causes compared with placebo."

items.assertion: Assertion: SGLT-2 inhibitors reduce hospitalization for heart failure in high-risk patients with T2DM.
items.reason: Reason: All completed SGLT-2 inhibitor cardiovascular outcomes trials have shown significant reduction in heart failure outcomes.
items.explanation: Both are true and the trial consistency supports the assertion.
items.reference: SGLT-2 Inhibitors: "All of the completed cardiovascular outcomes trials of SGLT-2 inhibitors have shown a significant reduction in heart failure outcomes with use of those agents."

items.assertion: Assertion: GLP-1 receptor agonists primarily reduce macroalbuminuria as their renal outcome benefit.
items.reason: Reason: GLP-1 receptor agonists reduce end-stage kidney disease more than SGLT-2 inhibitors in meta-analyses.
items.explanation: Assertion is true per meta-analyses; the reason reverses the predominant SGLT-2 inhibitor renal endpoint benefit.
items.reference: Renal Effects of GLP-1 Receptor Agonists and SGLT-2 Inhibitors: "the renal outcomes benefit noted in the trials of GLP-1 receptor agonists were primarily attributable to a reduction in macroalbuminuria, while SGLT-2 inhibitors were found to reduce the risks of worsening estimated glomerular filtration rate, end-stage kidney disease, or death due to renal causes."

items.assertion: Assertion: Metformin should not be the preferred initial step solely to reduce cardiovascular risk in a patient with established ASCVD.
items.reason: Reason: Metformin has no demonstrated efficacy in reducing cardiovascular risk in patients with established ASCVD.
items.explanation: Both are true; proven GLP-1 RA or SGLT-2 inhibitor therapy addresses residual risk.
items.reference: Case 1: "metformin has no demonstrated efficacy in the reduction of cardiovascular risk in patients with established ASCVD."

items.assertion: Assertion: Sitagliptin should be discontinued when liraglutide is started.
items.reason: Reason: Combined GLP-1 receptor agonist and DPP-4 inhibitor therapy is not recommended.
items.explanation: Both are true; substitution avoids redundant cost and unsupported combination use.
items.reference: Case 2: "This patient's sitagliptin should be discontinued when liraglutide is started, as combined use of a GLP-1 receptor agonist and a DPP-4 inhibitor is not recommended"

items.assertion: Assertion: Dulaglutide reduced MACE in REWIND despite most patients lacking prior cardiovascular events.
items.reason: Reason: REWIND only enrolled patients with prior myocardial infarction or stroke.
items.explanation: Assertion is true; REWIND uniquely included mostly patients without prior CV events—the reason is false.
items.reference: GLP-1 Receptor Agonists: "the REWIND trial of dulaglutide also enrolled patients at high risk, but in contrast to the other cardiovascular outcomes trials, most of the patients included had not had a prior cardiovascular event."

items.assertion: Assertion: Canagliflozin 100 mg daily is appropriate in T2DM with albuminuric nephropathy and eGFR ≥30 mL/min/1.73 m².
items.reason: Reason: CREDENCE demonstrated reduced kidney failure and cardiovascular events with canagliflozin versus placebo.
items.explanation: Both are true; CREDENCE underpins the labeled renal-cardiovascular indication.
items.reference: Renal Effects of GLP-1 Receptor Agonists and SGLT-2 Inhibitors: "canagliflozin, 100 mg daily, significantly reduced the risk of kidney failure and cardiovascular events at 2.62 years compared with placebo."

items.assertion: Assertion: Low-dose metformin should not be added in a patient with eGFR 30–44 mL/min/1.73 m² for cardio-renal protection.
items.reason: Reason: Metformin reduces progression to end-stage kidney disease in albuminuric diabetic nephropathy.
items.explanation: Assertion is true per Case 3; metformin is not recommended at this eGFR and lacks the renal outcomes shown for canagliflozin.
items.reference: Case 3: "the addition of even low-dosage metformin is not recommended in patients such as this with an estimated glomerular filtration rate between 30 and 44 mL/min per 1.73 m²."

items.assertion: Assertion: Dapagliflozin is the preferred next step in a patient with HFrEF and prediabetes.
items.reason: Reason: DAPA-HF showed dapagliflozin reduced worsening heart failure or cardiovascular death with or without diabetes.
items.explanation: Both are true; heart failure phenotype drives SGLT-2 inhibitor priority over lifestyle or metformin alone.
items.reference: Case 4: "the DAPA-HF trial found that in patients with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction with or without diabetes, dapagliflozin significantly reduced the risk of worsening heart failure or death of cardiovascular causes."
